# The Cyrillic Code III

> **Why does this matter?**
>
> You are about to cross a major threshold in your learning journey. By adding these nine consonants, you will know twenty-three letters in total, unlocking the ability to decode nearly all common street signs, menus, and basic words you encounter.

## Вступ — Introduction

Welcome to your third step in cracking the Cyrillic alphabet. You have already mastered the vowels and a solid foundation of essential consonants. Now, we are expanding your decodability even further, allowing you to read real-world vocabulary with much greater confidence. 

In this lesson, we will introduce nine new consonants. This brings your total to twenty-three letters. You will quickly notice that the more letters you learn, the faster you can recognize international words and essential everyday vocabulary. This progression transforms abstract symbols into meaningful language that you can immediately apply.

A key concept we will explore today is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds. Ukrainian consonants often come in matched pairs. One sound vibrates in your throat, which is called a voiced consonant. The other sound uses only air and no vibration, which is called a voiceless consonant. Understanding this mechanical difference is the secret to developing a natural and authentic accent. 

> [!tip]
> **Check your vibration**
> Place your hand lightly on your throat. When you make a voiced sound, you will feel a distinct buzz. When you make a voiceless sound, you will only feel air moving. This physical feedback is a powerful tool for self-correction.

Let us dive into the first group of new letters and see how many words you can already read.

## Приголосні — Consonants Б, Д, П

> — Це **банан** (This is a banana)?
> — Так, це **банан** (Yes, this is a banana).
> — А це **парк** (And this is a park)?
> — Ні, це **дім** (No, this is a home).

These three consonants are extremely common and will help you form many essential words used in daily life. Let us look at them closely and understand their unique characteristics.

The letter **Б** is a voiced consonant. Visually, it looks a bit like the number six, but it sounds exactly like the English equivalent. It is a foundational sound that you will encounter frequently. It is the first letter in the word **банан** (banana). Watch the pronunciation video below to hear it clearly and practice repeating it aloud.

### Літера Б
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Б](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V1hxBE_JbGg)

The letter **П** is the voiceless partner to **Б**. Be careful not to confuse it with the Greek symbol Pi or the letter for the "R" sound you learned previously. The Ukrainian **П** is crisp and completely unaspirated, meaning there is absolutely no puff of air when you say it. It starts the word **парк** (park). This lack of aspiration is crucial for sounding like a native speaker.

### Літера П
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — П](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JksSjjxyW5Y)

The letter **Д** is a voiced consonant. Its shape resembles a little house resting securely on a solid foundation. It sounds similar to the English equivalent, but there is a subtle difference in tongue placement. The tip of your tongue should touch your upper teeth rather than the roof of your mouth. You will see it in the high-frequency word **дім** (home).

### Літера Д
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Д](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g4Bh-lqzd48)

Let us practice reading some words using these letters. Read them aloud slowly, and pay close attention to the letters you already know from previous lessons.

| Word | Translation |
|---|---|
| **банан** | banana |
| **парк** | park |
| **дім** | home |
| **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building |

> [!warning]
> **False Friends**
> Remember that the letter you learned previously that looks like a Latin "P" actually makes the "R" sound. The new letter **П** makes the "P" sound. Contrast a word like **парк** (park) with words containing the "R" sound to solidify this distinction in your memory.

## Приголосні — Consonants З, Г, Х

> — Де **хліб** (Where is the bread)?
> — **Хліб** тут (The bread is here).
> — А **газета** (And the newspaper)?
> — **Газета** там (The newspaper is there).

Now we will look at three more consonants that expand your range of expression. These include a sharp hissing sound and two sounds that are formed deeper in your mouth and throat. Mastering these will significantly improve your overall pronunciation.

The letter **З** is a voiced consonant. It looks strikingly similar to the number three. It makes a clear buzzing sound, completely identical to the first sound in the English word "zebra." We find it in the everyday word **зуб** (tooth). Focus on sustaining the vibration when you pronounce it.

### Літера З
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — З](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BhASNxitC1A)

The letter **Г** is voiced, but it might be the trickiest letter in this group for English speakers. It is a soft, throaty sound. It is definitively not a hard sound like in the English word "go." Instead, it sounds like a voiced glottal fricative, almost like a heavy, vibrating sigh. You can practice it with the word **газета** (newspaper).

### Літера Г
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Г](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gVnclpSI0DU)

The letter **Х** is the voiceless counterpart to the sound above. Visually, it looks exactly like the Latin "X," but it absolutely does not make a "ks" sound. Instead, it sounds like the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch" or the German "Bach." A culturally important word starting with this letter is **хліб** (bread). 

### Літера Х
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Х](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vpr58zJSJKc)

Let us look at these letters in context. Notice how they combine smoothly with the vowels you have already mastered to form recognizable vocabulary. 

| Word | Translation |
|---|---|
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **газета** | newspaper |
| **хліб** | bread |

> [!culture]
> **The Importance of Bread**
> The word **хліб** (bread) is deeply foundational and revered in Ukrainian culture. Bread is a timeless symbol of hospitality, life, community, and prosperity. Conveniently, you can now read this essential word completely using the letters you have learned!

## Дзвінкі та глухі — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs

> — Що це (What is this)?
> — Це **шапка** (This is a hat).
> — А це (And this)?
> — Це **школа** (This is a school).

We have extensively mentioned voiced and voiceless pairs throughout this lesson. This phonetic concept means that your mouth, lips, and tongue are in the exact same physical position, but one sound uses active vocal cord vibration while the other uses only a stream of air. We will now learn three distinctive sounds collectively called "hushers" that perfectly demonstrate this system.

The letter **Ж** is voiced. Visually, it looks quite a bit like a six-legged beetle, and appropriately, it makes a rich buzzing sound similar to a flying insect. The sound is very close to the middle consonant in the English word "measure." Ensure your lips are slightly rounded when you produce it.

### Літера Ж
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ж](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM)

The letter **Ш** is the voiceless partner to **Ж**. It looks somewhat like a pitchfork pointing squarely upwards. It makes a strong "sh" sound, much like in the English word "ship." However, your tongue should be pulled back slightly compared to the English equivalent for a more authentic tone. You will use it very often, such as in the word **школа** (school).

### Літера Ш
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ш](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY)

The letter **Ч** is also voiceless. It visually resembles an upside-down chair or a handwritten number four. It makes a sharp "ch" sound, similar to the beginning of the English word "church." It appears frequently in basic vocabulary, such as in the word **час** (time) and the universally recognized word **чай** (tea). 

### Літера Ч
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ч](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA)

Let us summarize the vital pairs we have learned so far. Notice how elegantly they match up perfectly within the phonetic system.

- **Б** (voiced) pairs directly with **П** (voiceless).
- **Д** (voiced) pairs directly with the letter for the "T" sound (voiceless).
- **З** (voiced) pairs directly with the letter for the "S" sound (voiceless).
- **Ж** (voiced) pairs directly with **Ш** (voiceless).

> [!did-you-know]
> **Visual Mnemonics**
> Culturally, Ukrainian children are very often taught that the letter **Ж** looks exactly like a little bug (**жук**), and the sound it makes mimics the loud buzzing of an insect. This is a very authentic, widely used, and helpful memory trick!

## Практика читання — Reading Practice

> — Де **транспорт** (Where is the transport)?
> — **Транспорт** там (The transport is there).
> — А де **пошта** (And where is the post office)?
> — **Пошта** тут (The post office is here).

Now that you have twenty-three robust letters securely in your mental toolkit, you can confidently read a vast amount of practical vocabulary. This exciting stage includes many international words that you will immediately recognize once you sound out the letters sequentially. 

Let us practice reading some new words together. Take your time, focus on accuracy, and sound out each individual letter carefully. Remember the consonant pairs we discussed and consciously try to feel the vibration in your throat for the voiced consonants.

| Ukrainian | Translation |
|---|---|
| **транспорт** | transport |
| **час** | time |
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **чай** | tea |
| **школа** | school |

You can also read simple descriptive noun phrases now. Try reading these short combinations aloud to practice fluidity and rhythm.

- **цей будинок** — this building
- **той хліб** — that bread
- **той парк** — that park
- **ця газета** — this newspaper

Notice exactly how the words feel physically in your mouth as you transition between sounds. The clear distinction between a soft throat sound and a sharp hissing sound is precisely what gives the language its beautifully unique rhythm. You are actively building essential muscle memory every single time you read aloud. Keep practicing these phrases until they feel entirely natural and effortless.

## Підсумок — Summary

You have made absolutely incredible progress in this session. You can now reliably recognize and accurately pronounce twenty-three of the thirty-three letters in the entire alphabet. This is a massive milestone in your language acquisition journey.

This impressive collection includes six foundational vowels and seventeen versatile consonants. You also now deeply understand the crucial mechanical difference between voiced sounds that vibrate and voiceless sounds that only use a stream of air. This mechanical understanding will significantly and permanently improve your accent.

Let us perform a final, comprehensive decodability check. Can you read and instantly translate the following essential words?

- **парк** (park)
- **хліб** (bread)
- **шапка** (hat)
- **пошта** (post office)

In the next and final module of this foundational series, we will introduce the remaining ten characters, including the soft vowels and special pronunciation symbols, successfully completing your journey to full alphabet mastery.

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests. It is the language of action, heavily used in classrooms, streets, and daily conversations. Adding simple polite words transforms direct commands into respectful requests.

## Introduction: The Power of Commands and Requests

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. It is highly practical for everyday life and represents the core language of action. In Ukrainian, the imperative mood is dynamic and frequently appears in classroom instructions, street conversations, and casual gatherings.

> — **Слуха́й!** — Listen!
> — **Слуха́ю.** — I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.

The examples above show direct instructions. You do not just describe the action; you actively prompt the other person to act. The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

- **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
- **Допоможи́!** — Help!
- **Покажи́!** — Show!
- **Чека́й!** — Wait!
- **Сті́й!** — Stop!

Sometimes, you need to use the imperative mood for classroom instructions or when reacting to immediate, fast-paced events. The shortest commands are usually informal and direct.

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please). This single phrase instantly changes a strong order into a culturally appropriate request. You can place it at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.

- **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends, family members, children, and people your own age in casual settings. We form this imperative by taking the verb and applying the command ending. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **чита́ти** | **чита́й** | read |
| **слу́хати** | **слу́хай** | listen |
| **чека́ти** | **чека́й** | wait |

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). The **-и** ending ensures the word remains clear and distinguishable.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **писа́ти** | **пиши́** | write |
| **іти́** | **іди́** | go |
| **сказа́ти** | **скажи́** | say |
| **допомогти́** | **допоможи́** | help |

- **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
- **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
- **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.

> — **Чита́й це́.** — Read this.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Та́к.** — Yes.

| Informal Command (**ти**) | Formal/Plural Command (**ви**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **чита́й** | **чита́йте** | read |
| **слу́хай** | **слу́хайте** | listen |
| **чека́й** | **чека́йте** | wait |
| **пиши́** | **пиші́ть** | write |
| **іди́** | **іді́ть** | go |

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.

- **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!

> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.

| Pronoun | Command | English |
|---|---|---|
| **ти** | **диви́сь** | look (informal) |
| **ви** | **диві́ться** | look (formal/plural) |

- **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
- **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
- **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

| Infinitive | Command (**ти**) | Command (**ви**) | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| **да́ти** | **да́й** | **да́йте** | give |
| **взя́ти** | **візьми́** | **візьмі́ть** | take |
| **стоя́ти** | **сті́й** | **сті́йте** | stand / stop |
| **показа́ти** | **покажи́** | **покажі́ть** | show |

- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
- **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
- **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
- **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
- **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.

> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?

- **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
- **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.

> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action. For example, the perfective verb **сказа́ти** asks for a specific, immediate answer, so the command **скажи́** focuses on the final result of the action rather than the continuous process.

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions, make polite requests, and establish boundaries. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing, reflecting the social context of the conversation. You use the shorter form for informal interactions with friends (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite and respectful in any scenario.

- **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
- **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal/plural)
- **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
- **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal/plural)
- **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener before giving a command.

---

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests. It is the language of action, heavily used in classrooms, streets, and daily conversations. Adding simple polite words transforms direct commands into respectful requests.

## Introduction: The Power of Commands and Requests

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. It is highly practical for everyday life and represents the core language of action. In Ukrainian, the imperative mood is dynamic and frequently appears in classroom instructions, street conversations, and casual gatherings.

> — **Слуха́й!** — Listen!
> — **Слуха́ю.** — I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.

The examples above show direct instructions. You do not just describe the action; you actively prompt the other person to act. The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

- **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
- **Допоможи́!** — Help!
- **Покажи́!** — Show!
- **Чека́й!** — Wait!
- **Сті́й!** — Stop!

Sometimes, you need to use the imperative mood for classroom instructions or when reacting to immediate, fast-paced events. The shortest commands are usually informal and direct.

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please). This single phrase instantly changes a strong order into a culturally appropriate request. You can place it at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.

- **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.

The mood relies heavily on the specific situation and social context. When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. When you address a group or an unfamiliar adult, the form changes significantly to reflect social distance.

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential in Ukrainian society. Even if the grammatical form remains direct, adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful in a café, a store, or on the street. It demonstrates good manners.

By mastering these short, effective phrases, you gain a vital tool for interacting directly with others and navigating daily situations in Ukrainian.

|---|---|---|
| **чита́ти** | **чита́й** | read |
| **слу́хати** | **слу́хай** | listen |
| **чека́ти** | **чека́й** | wait |

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). The **-и** ending ensures the word remains clear and distinguishable.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **писа́ти** | **пиши́** | write |
| **іти́** | **іди́** | go |
| **сказа́ти** | **скажи́** | say |
| **допомогти́** | **допоможи́** | help |

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there) to give clear directions.

- **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
- **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
- **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.

- **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests. It is the language of action, heavily used in classrooms, streets, and daily conversations. Adding simple polite words transforms direct commands into respectful requests.

## Introduction: The Power of Commands and Requests

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. It is highly practical for everyday life and represents the core language of action. In Ukrainian, the imperative mood is dynamic and frequently appears in classroom instructions, street conversations, and casual gatherings.

> — **Слуха́й!** — Listen!
> — **Слуха́ю.** — I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.

The examples above show direct instructions. You do not just describe the action; you actively prompt the other person to act. The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

- **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
- **Допоможи́!** — Help!
- **Покажи́!** — Show!
- **Чека́й!** — Wait!
- **Сті́й!** — Stop!

Sometimes, you need to use the imperative mood for classroom instructions or when reacting to immediate, fast-paced events. The shortest commands are usually informal and direct.

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please). This single phrase instantly changes a strong order into a culturally appropriate request. You can place it at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.

- **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.

The mood relies heavily on the specific situation and social context. When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. When you address a group or an unfamiliar adult, the form changes significantly to reflect social distance.

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential in Ukrainian society. Even if the grammatical form remains direct, adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful in a café, a store, or on the street. It demonstrates good manners.

By mastering these short, effective phrases, you gain a vital tool for interacting directly with others and navigating daily situations in Ukrainian.

## The Informal Imperative: Addressing 'Ти'

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends, family members, children, and people your own age in casual settings. We form this imperative by taking the verb and applying the command ending. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **чита́ти** | **чита́й** | read |
| **слу́хати** | **слу́хай** | listen |
| **чека́ти** | **чека́й** | wait |

For verbs like **чита́ти**, **слу́хати**, and **чека́ти**, the stem ends in a vowel. In these cases, we simply attach the letter **-й**. This creates a smooth sound that is easy to pronounce.

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). The **-и** ending ensures the word remains clear and distinguishable.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **писа́ти** | **пиши́** | write |
| **іти́** | **іди́** | go |
| **сказа́ти** | **скажи́** | say |
| **допомогти́** | **допоможи́** | help |

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there) to give clear directions.

- **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
- **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
- **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.

Let us see how people use these informal commands in everyday life. In this scenario, two friends are studying together and exchanging quick instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́.** — Read this.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Та́к.** — Yes.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

Notice how one person gives a command, and the other responds with a present tense verb describing their action. The form **пиши́** directly prompts the friend to act. It creates a natural, interactive dialogue.

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**). This phonetic rule helps maintain a natural flow of speech.

By remembering these two basic endings, you can build many commands for friends and family, allowing you to interact comfortably in relaxed environments.

## The Formal and Plural Imperative: Addressing 'Ви'

When speaking to a group of people or addressing a single person respectfully, you use the formal or plural command form. This form corresponds directly to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You construct this formal imperative effortlessly by adding the suffix **-те** to the informal imperative you just learned.

| Informal Command (**ти**) | Formal/Plural Command (**ви**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **чита́й** | **чита́йте** | read |
| **слу́хай** | **слу́хайте** | listen |
| **чека́й** | **чека́йте** | wait |
| **пиши́** | **пиші́ть** | write |
| **іди́** | **іді́ть** | go |

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.

Using the **-те** suffix adds an instant layer of politeness and formality to your speech. If you are in a new place, speaking to an older person, or talking to a stranger on the street, you must choose the **ви** form. Failing to do so can sound extremely rude and disrespectful.

- **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
- **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
- **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!

We also use this form when a teacher addresses a classroom full of students, as the teacher is speaking to multiple people simultaneously.

> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.

<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern in the formal imperative. For example, the verb **диви́тися** (to look) is reflexive. For the informal form, we use **диви́сь**. For the formal or plural form, we say **диві́ться**. Notice how the reflexive ending shifts slightly to accommodate the plural marker.

| Pronoun | Command | English |
|---|---|---|
| **ти** | **диви́сь** | look (informal) |
| **ви** | **диві́ться** | look (formal/plural) |

- **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
- **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
- **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. English uses the base verb, but Ukrainian requires you to adapt the verb into the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**) so it targets the person directly.

When you interact with native speakers in professional or public settings, paying close attention to the formal **-те** ending will greatly improve your communication skills and demonstrate your cultural awareness.

## Special Verbs and Reflexives

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms that do not perfectly follow the standard patterns. You simply need to memorize them because they appear so often. The verbs **да́ти** (to give), **взя́ти** (to take), and **стоя́ти** (to stand) fall into this special group.

| Infinitive | Command (**ти**) | Command (**ви**) | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| **да́ти** | **да́й** | **да́йте** | give |
| **взя́ти** | **візьми́** | **візьмі́ть** | take |
| **стоя́ти** | **сті́й** | **сті́йте** | stand / stop |
| **показа́ти** | **покажи́** | **покажі́ть** | show |

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping, asking for directions, or navigating public spaces.

- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
- **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
- **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
- **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
- **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.

> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions and tell someone not to do something. We simply add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb. The verb form itself does not change.

- **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
- **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
- **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action. For example, the perfective verb **сказа́ти** asks for a specific, immediate answer, so the command **скажи́** focuses on the final result of the action rather than the continuous process.

Understanding these special verbs and negative structures allows you to express precise needs and boundaries.

## Summary and Checkpoint

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions, make polite requests, and establish boundaries. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing, reflecting the social context of the conversation. You use the shorter form for informal interactions with friends (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite and respectful in any scenario.

- **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
- **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal/plural)
- **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
- **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal/plural)
- **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener before giving a command.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

+- **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
+- **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
+- **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
 
-Here is an example of formal commands used when asking someone for directions or instructing a group:
+We also use this form when a teacher addresses a classroom full of students, as the teacher is speaking to multiple people simultaneously.
 
-> — Слухайте мене, будь ласка!
-> — Ми слухаємо. Що там?
-> — Ось слово.
-> — Яке слово?
-> — Читайте!
-> — Ми не читаємо. Покажіть ще.
+> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
+> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
+> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
+> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
+> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.
 
-| Ukrainian | English |
-|---|---|
-| Слухайте мене, будь ласка! | Listen to me, please! |
-| Ми слухаємо. Що там? | We are listening. What is there? |
-| Ось слово. | Here is a word. |
-| Яке слово? | Which word? |
-| Читайте! | Read! |
-| Ми не читаємо. Покажіть ще. | We are not reading. Show more. |
+<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->
 
-You can also use negative formal commands to instruct groups on what not to do. This is very common for safety warnings or classroom rules:
+Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern in the formal imperative. For example, the verb **диви́тися** (to look) is reflexive. For the informal form, we use **диви́сь**. For the formal or plural form, we say **диві́ться**. Notice how the reflexive ending shifts slightly to accommodate the plural marker.
 
-- **Не дивіться там.** — Do not look there.
-- **Не ідіть тут.** — Do not go here.
+| Pronoun | Command | English |
+|---|---|---|
+| **ти** | **диви́сь** | look (informal) |
+| **ви** | **диві́ться** | look (formal/plural) |
 
-Whenever you are unsure of the relationship, it is always safer to use the formal **ви** command to avoid sounding disrespectful.
+- **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
+- **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
+- **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.
 
+> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
+> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. English uses the base verb, but Ukrainian requires you to adapt the verb into the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**) so it targets the person directly.
+
+When you interact with native speakers in professional or public settings, paying close attention to the formal **-те** ending will greatly improve your communication skills and demonstrate your cultural awareness.
+
 ## Special Verbs and Reflexives
 
-Some very high-frequency verbs have irregular or unique command forms. Because you will hear these every day, it is best to simply memorize them. For example, **дати** (to give) becomes **дай** or **дайте**, and **сказати** (to say) becomes **скажи** or **скажіть**.
+Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms that do not perfectly follow the standard patterns. You simply need to memorize them because they appear so often. The verbs **да́ти** (to give), **взя́ти** (to take), and **стоя́ти** (to stand) fall into this special group.
 
-| Command for one person | Command for multiple people | English meaning |
-|---|---|---|
-| **скажи** | **скажіть** | Say! / Tell! |
-| **дай** | **дайте** | Give! |
-| **покажи** | **покажіть** | Show! |
-| **допоможи** | **допоможіть** | Help! |
-| **візьми** | **візьміть** | Take! |
+| Infinitive | Command (**ти**) | Command (**ви**) | English |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| **да́ти** | **да́й** | **да́йте** | give |
+| **взя́ти** | **візьми́** | **візьмі́ть** | take |
+| **стоя́ти** | **сті́й** | **сті́йте** | stand / stop |
+| **показа́ти** | **покажи́** | **покажі́ть** | show |
 
-You should also notice the verb **дивитися** (to look). It is a reflexive verb, which means it ends in **-ся** or **-сь**. In the command form, the ending changes. It becomes **дивись** for one person and **дивіться** for multiple people.
+You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping, asking for directions, or navigating public spaces.
 
-Here is a dialogue using these special verbs when someone needs help:
+- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
+- **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
+- **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
+- **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
+- **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
 
-> — Допоможіть, будь ласка!
-> — Я тут. Що дати?
-> — Дайте, будь ласка, це.
-> — Візьміть. Ще що?
-> — Покажіть, будь ласка, слова.
-> — Які слова?
-> — Ті слова. Ось.
+> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
+> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
+> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
+> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
 
-| Ukrainian | English |
-|---|---|
-| Допоможіть, будь ласка! | Please help! |
-| Я тут. Що дати? | I am here. What to give? |
-| Дайте, будь ласка, це. | Please give this. |
-| Візьміть. Ще що? | Take it. What else? |
-| Покажіть, будь ласка, слова. | Please show the words. |
-| Які слова? | Which words? |
-| Ті слова. Ось. | Those words. Here. |
+<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->
 
-And you can also make these negative when necessary:
+You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions and tell someone not to do something. We simply add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb. The verb form itself does not change.
 
-- **Не кажи це.** — Do not say this.
-- **Не беріть те.** — Do not take that.
-- **Не дай це.** — Do not give this.
+- **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
+- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
+- **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
+- **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
 
+> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
+> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action. For example, the perfective verb **сказа́ти** asks for a specific, immediate answer, so the command **скажи́** focuses on the final result of the action rather than the continuous process.
+
+Understanding these special verbs and negative structures allows you to express precise needs and boundaries.
+
 ## Summary and Checkpoint
 
-In this lesson, we learned how to give commands, make polite requests, and tell people what not to do using the imperative mood.
+The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions, make polite requests, and establish boundaries. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing, reflecting the social context of the conversation. You use the shorter form for informal interactions with friends (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite and respectful in any scenario.
 
-- Drop the ending from the dictionary form to create an informal command for one person (**-й** or **-и**). This makes your speech direct and personal.
-- Add endings to create formal or plural commands (**-те** or **-іть**). This is required when speaking to groups or to show respect to someone older or unfamiliar.
-- Use special polite words, like the universal phrase for "please," to make your commands softer and more respectful in daily situations.
-- Add a negative word (**не**) in front of the action word to create a prohibition, telling someone not to do an action.
+- **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
+- **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal/plural)
+- **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
+- **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal/plural)
+- **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
+- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
 
-Let's review what we have learned with some self-check questions. Check if you can convert infinitives to commands correctly:
+Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener before giving a command.
 
-1. How do you change a command for one person to a command for multiple people?
-   *You add **-те** to the informal command (e.g., **читай** → **читайте**).*
-2. What are the command forms of the words for "to give" and "to say"?
-   *These are high-frequency irregular forms: **дай / дайте** and **скажи / скажіть**.*
-3. How do you make a command negative?
-   *You put the negative word directly before the command word.*
-4. Where can you place the polite phrase "please" in a sentence?
-   *It can go almost anywhere, but often comes right after the command.*
+---
 
-Keep practicing these essential command words — they are the foundation for everyday interactions in Ukrainian!
+# Підсумок
+
+The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.
+
+- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
+- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
+- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
+- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**
\ No newline at end of file

<!-- SCOPE

# Наказовий спосіб (Imperative and Requests)

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> When you need someone to listen, stop, or help, you must know how to give commands. The imperative mood is essential for direct communication and polite requests in daily situations.

## Introduction: The Power of Commands and Requests

To tell someone to do something, you use the imperative mood. Commands take two forms depending on who you are addressing. You use one form when speaking informally to one person, like a friend or a child. You use a different form when speaking to multiple people or when speaking formally to one person, such as a teacher or a stranger.

> [!tip]
> Do not confuse a general instruction with a personal command. If you see **Читати текст** (Read the text) in exam instructions, it uses the dictionary form as a general rule. But if you want to tell your friend to read, you must say **Читай!** The dictionary form is for impersonal instructions, and the command form is for people.

Politeness in Ukrainian is built directly into the grammar of commands. The formal command is not just for multiple people — it is also how you show respect to a single person. Speaking to a stranger, a teacher, or someone older requires the formal command. If you use the informal command with someone you don't know well, it sounds rude.

The most important phrase for politeness is **будь ласка** (please). This is a universal phrase that makes any command sound like a polite request. You can place it almost anywhere in a sentence, but it is very common to put it right after the command word.

Let's compare how direct commands and polite requests sound in short classroom situations. Notice how we use the formal plural form to address the class.

| Ukrainian | English |
|---|---|
| Читати — це добре. | To read is good. |
| Читай тут, будь ласка! | Read here, please! |
| Чекати там. | To wait there. |
| Чекайте там, будь ласка. | Wait there, please. |
| Слухати слово. | To listen to a word. |
| Слухайте слово, будь ласка. | Listen to the word, please. |

These commands are the foundation of daily communication. A teacher will use them to guide a class, and you will use them with friends to ask for things. Practice saying these short commands out loud so you can recognize them instantly when you hear them. By the end of this lesson, you will be able to recognize the imperative in everyday classroom instructions and confidently soften your own commands into requests.

## The Informal Imperative: Addressing 'Ти'

The command form can look a little different from the dictionary form. For the informal **ти** (you singular), commands typically end in either **-й** or **-и**. Let's look at the patterns for some common actions. 

When you address a close friend, a family member, or a child, you always use the informal imperative. It creates a warm, personal connection. Using formal language with close friends can actually feel distant or strange in Ukrainian.

| Dictionary Form | Command for one person (ти) | English meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **писати** | **пиши** | Write! |
| **іти** | **іди** | Go! |
| **дивитися** | **дивись** | Look! |
| **чекати** | **чекай** | Wait! |

Here is a short conversation showing informal commands between friends who are trying to meet up. Notice the use of **іди** (go) and **дивись** (look).

> — Іди до нас!
> — Де ви?
> — Ми тут. Дивись!
> — Я іду. Що ви пишете?
> — Це слова. Пиши також!
> — Я не пишу. Я чекаю.
> — Пиши тут!
> — Так, я пишу.

| Ukrainian | English |
|---|---|
| Іди до нас! | Come to us! |
| Де ви? | Where are you? |
| Ми тут. Дивись! | We are here. Look! |
| Я іду. Що ви пишете? | I am coming. What are you writing? |
| Це слова. Пиши також! | These are words. Write too! |
| Я не пишу. Я чекаю. | I am not writing. I am waiting. |
| Пиши тут! | Write here! |
| Так, я пишу. | Yes, I am writing. |

Notice how natural it is to switch between telling a friend what to do and describing what you are doing yourself. You can also make negative commands by placing **не** (not) in front of the verb. This is very useful when you want to stop a friend from doing something or when you are giving directions on what to avoid.

- **Не слухай це.** — Do not listen to this.
- **Не чекай там.** — Do not wait there.

Here is another brief exchange using negative informal commands:

> — Я чекаю тут.
> — Не чекай тут.
> — Чому ні?
> — Там людина. Не слухай!
> — Добре.

| Ukrainian | English |
|---|---|
| Я чекаю тут. | I am waiting here. |
| Не чекай тут. | Do not wait here. |
| Чому ні? | Why not? |
| Там людина. Не слухай! | There is a person. Do not listen! |
| Добре. | Okay. |

These informal commands are essential for building close relationships. You will hear them constantly in daily life, whether someone is telling you to look at something interesting or asking you to wait for a moment.

## The Formal and Plural Imperative: Addressing 'Ви'

When you are addressing more than one person, or when you are speaking to a single person formally (like a teacher, an older person, or a stranger), you must use the **ви** form of the imperative. Notice that the formal or plural command simply adds **-те** to the informal command. For example, **читай** becomes **читайте**, and **пиши** becomes **пишіть**.

| Dictionary Form | Command for group/formal (ви) | English meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **читати** | **читайте** | Read! |
| **писати** | **пишіть** | Write! |
| **стояти** | **стійте** | Stand! |

Let's compare how you change a statement into a direct formal command. This is especially useful in professional settings or when addressing a group:

| Statement | Command for group/polite | English translation |
|---|---|---|
| **Ви читаєте.** | **Читайте!** | You read. / Read! |
| **Ви пишете слова.** | **Пишіть слова!** | You write words. / Write words! |
| **Ви стоїте.** | **Стійте!** | You stand. / Stand! |

> [!culture]
> In a Ukrainian classroom, a teacher will often use the formal plural command to address the entire class, like **Читайте** (Read). But when speaking to one student, the teacher will use the informal singular command, like **Читай**. This is normal and shows a warm, direct connection with the student. Contrastive pairs like this highlight the social feel of the language. Using **скажи** is friendly, but **скажіть, будь ласка** shows respect.

Here is an example of formal commands used when asking someone for directions or instructing a group:

> — Слухайте мене, будь ласка!
> — Ми слухаємо. Що там?
> — Ось слово.
> — Яке слово?
> — Читайте!
> — Ми не читаємо. Покажіть ще.

| Ukrainian | English |
|---|---|
| Слухайте мене, будь ласка! | Listen to me, please! |
| Ми слухаємо. Що там? | We are listening. What is there? |
| Ось слово. | Here is a word. |
| Яке слово? | Which word? |
| Читайте! | Read! |
| Ми не читаємо. Покажіть ще. | We are not reading. Show more. |

You can also use negative formal commands to instruct groups on what not to do. This is very common for safety warnings or classroom rules:

- **Не дивіться там.** — Do not look there.
- **Не ідіть тут.** — Do not go here.

Whenever you are unsure of the relationship, it is always safer to use the formal **ви** command to avoid sounding disrespectful.

## Special Verbs and Reflexives

Some very high-frequency verbs have irregular or unique command forms. Because you will hear these every day, it is best to simply memorize them. For example, **дати** (to give) becomes **дай** or **дайте**, and **сказати** (to say) becomes **скажи** or **скажіть**.

| Command for one person | Command for multiple people | English meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **скажи** | **скажіть** | Say! / Tell! |
| **дай** | **дайте** | Give! |
| **покажи** | **покажіть** | Show! |
| **допоможи** | **допоможіть** | Help! |
| **візьми** | **візьміть** | Take! |

You should also notice the verb **дивитися** (to look). It is a reflexive verb, which means it ends in **-ся** or **-сь**. In the command form, the ending changes. It becomes **дивись** for one person and **дивіться** for multiple people.

Here is a dialogue using these special verbs when someone needs help:

> — Допоможіть, будь ласка!
> — Я тут. Що дати?
> — Дайте, будь ласка, це.
> — Візьміть. Ще що?
> — Покажіть, будь ласка, слова.
> — Які слова?
> — Ті слова. Ось.

| Ukrainian | English |
|---|---|
| Допоможіть, будь ласка! | Please help! |
| Я тут. Що дати? | I am here. What to give? |
| Дайте, будь ласка, це. | Please give this. |
| Візьміть. Ще що? | Take it. What else? |
| Покажіть, будь ласка, слова. | Please show the words. |
| Які слова? | Which words? |
| Ті слова. Ось. | Those words. Here. |

And you can also make these negative when necessary:

- **Не кажи це.** — Do not say this.
- **Не беріть те.** — Do not take that.
- **Не дай це.** — Do not give this.

## Summary and Checkpoint

In this lesson, we learned how to give commands, make polite requests, and tell people what not to do using the imperative mood.

- Drop the ending from the dictionary form to create an informal command for one person (**-й** or **-и**). This makes your speech direct and personal.
- Add endings to create formal or plural commands (**-те** or **-іть**). This is required when speaking to groups or to show respect to someone older or unfamiliar.
- Use special polite words, like the universal phrase for "please," to make your commands softer and more respectful in daily situations.
- Add a negative word (**не**) in front of the action word to create a prohibition, telling someone not to do an action.

Let's review what we have learned with some self-check questions. Check if you can convert infinitives to commands correctly:

1. How do you change a command for one person to a command for multiple people?
   *You add **-те** to the informal command (e.g., **читай** → **читайте**).*
2. What are the command forms of the words for "to give" and "to say"?
   *These are high-frequency irregular forms: **дай / дайте** and **скажи / скажіть**.*
3. How do you make a command negative?
   *You put the negative word directly before the command word.*
4. Where can you place the polite phrase "please" in a sentence?
   *It can go almost anywhere, but often comes right after the command.*

Keep practicing these essential command words — they are the foundation for everyday interactions in Ukrainian!

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests. It is the language of action, heavily used in classrooms, streets, and daily conversations. Adding simple polite words transforms direct commands into respectful requests.

## Introduction: The Power of Commands and Requests

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. It is highly practical for everyday life and represents the core language of action. In Ukrainian, the imperative mood is dynamic and frequently appears in classroom instructions, street conversations, and casual gatherings.

> — **Слуха́й!** — Listen!
> — **Слуха́ю.** — I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.

The examples above show direct instructions. You do not just describe the action; you actively prompt the other person to act. The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

- **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
- **Допоможи́!** — Help!
- **Покажи́!** — Show!
- **Чека́й!** — Wait!
- **Сті́й!** — Stop!

Sometimes, you need to use the imperative mood for classroom instructions or when reacting to immediate, fast-paced events. The shortest commands are usually informal and direct.

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please). This single phrase instantly changes a strong order into a culturally appropriate request. You can place it at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.

- **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.

The mood relies heavily on the specific situation and social context. When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. When you address a group or an unfamiliar adult, the form changes significantly to reflect social distance.

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential in Ukrainian society. Even if the grammatical form remains direct, adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful in a café, a store, or on the street. It demonstrates good manners.

By mastering these short, effective phrases, you gain a vital tool for interacting directly with others and navigating daily situations in Ukrainian.

## The Informal Imperative: Addressing 'Ти'

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends, family members, children, and people your own age in casual settings. We form this imperative by taking the verb and applying the command ending. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **чита́ти** | **чита́й** | read |
| **слу́хати** | **слу́хай** | listen |
| **чека́ти** | **чека́й** | wait |

For verbs like **чита́ти**, **слу́хати**, and **чека́ти**, the stem ends in a vowel. In these cases, we simply attach the letter **-й**. This creates a smooth sound that is easy to pronounce.

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). The **-и** ending ensures the word remains clear and distinguishable.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **писа́ти** | **пиши́** | write |
| **іти́** | **іди́** | go |
| **сказа́ти** | **скажи́** | say |
| **допомогти́** | **допоможи́** | help |

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there) to give clear directions.

- **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
- **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
- **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.

Let us see how people use these informal commands in everyday life. In this scenario, two friends are studying together and exchanging quick instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́.** — Read this.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Та́к.** — Yes.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

Notice how one person gives a command, and the other responds with a present tense verb describing their action. The form **пиши́** directly prompts the friend to act. It creates a natural, interactive dialogue.

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**). This phonetic rule helps maintain a natural flow of speech.

By remembering these two basic endings, you can build many commands for friends and family, allowing you to interact comfortably in relaxed environments.

## The Formal and Plural Imperative: Addressing 'Ви'

When speaking to a group of people or addressing a single person respectfully, you use the formal or plural command form. This form corresponds directly to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You construct this formal imperative effortlessly by adding the suffix **-те** to the informal imperative you just learned.

| Informal Command (**ти**) | Formal/Plural Command (**ви**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **чита́й** | **чита́йте** | read |
| **слу́хай** | **слу́хайте** | listen |
| **чека́й** | **чека́йте** | wait |
| **пиши́** | **пиші́ть** | write |
| **іди́** | **іді́ть** | go |

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.

Using the **-те** suffix adds an instant layer of politeness and formality to your speech. If you are in a new place, speaking to an older person, or talking to a stranger on the street, you must choose the **ви** form. Failing to do so can sound extremely rude and disrespectful.

- **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
- **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
- **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!

We also use this form when a teacher addresses a classroom full of students, as the teacher is speaking to multiple people simultaneously.

> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.

<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern in the formal imperative. For example, the verb **диви́тися** (to look) is reflexive. For the informal form, we use **диви́сь**. For the formal or plural form, we say **диві́ться**. Notice how the reflexive ending shifts slightly to accommodate the plural marker.

| Pronoun | Command | English |
|---|---|---|
| **ти** | **диви́сь** | look (informal) |
| **ви** | **диві́ться** | look (formal/plural) |

- **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
- **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
- **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. English uses the base verb, but Ukrainian requires you to adapt the verb into the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**) so it targets the person directly.

When you interact with native speakers in professional or public settings, paying close attention to the formal **-те** ending will greatly improve your communication skills and demonstrate your cultural awareness.

## Special Verbs and Reflexives

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms that do not perfectly follow the standard patterns. You simply need to memorize them because they appear so often. The verbs **да́ти** (to give), **взя́ти** (to take), and **стоя́ти** (to stand) fall into this special group.

| Infinitive | Command (**ти**) | Command (**ви**) | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| **да́ти** | **да́й** | **да́йте** | give |
| **взя́ти** | **візьми́** | **візьмі́ть** | take |
| **стоя́ти** | **сті́й** | **сті́йте** | stand / stop |
| **показа́ти** | **покажи́** | **покажі́ть** | show |

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping, asking for directions, or navigating public spaces.

- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
- **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
- **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
- **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
- **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.

> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions and tell someone not to do something. We simply add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb. The verb form itself does not change.

- **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
- **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
- **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action. For example, the perfective verb **сказа́ти** asks for a specific, immediate answer, so the command **скажи́** focuses on the final result of the action rather than the continuous process.

Understanding these special verbs and negative structures allows you to express precise needs and boundaries.

## Summary and Checkpoint

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions, make polite requests, and establish boundaries. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing, reflecting the social context of the conversation. You use the shorter form for informal interactions with friends (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite and respectful in any scenario.

- **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
- **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal/plural)
- **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
- **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal/plural)
- **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener before giving a command.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Form imperatives from required verbs, polite requests
Not covered:
  - Complex directions → a1-48
-->

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests. It is the language of action, heavily used in classrooms, streets, and daily conversations. Adding simple polite words transforms direct commands into respectful requests.

## Introduction: Asking for Action

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. It is highly practical for everyday life and represents the core language of action. In Ukrainian, the imperative mood is dynamic and frequently appears in classroom instructions, street conversations, and casual gatherings.

> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Listen, please!
> — **Та́к, я слу́хаю.** — Yes, I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Та́к, я дивлю́сь.** — Yes, I am looking.
> — **Ду́же до́бре.** — Very good.

The examples above show direct instructions. You do not just describe the action; you actively prompt the other person to act. The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

- **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
- **Допоможи́!** — Help!
- **Покажи́!** — Show!
- **Чека́й!** — Wait!
- **Сті́й!** — Stop!
- **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
- **Чита́й це́!** — Read this!
- **Пиши́ ту́т!** — Write here!

Sometimes, you need to use the imperative mood for classroom instructions or when reacting to immediate, fast-paced events. The shortest commands are usually informal and direct.

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please). This single phrase instantly changes a strong order into a culturally appropriate request. You can place it at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.

- **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
- **Покажи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please show.

The mood relies heavily on the specific situation and social context. When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. When you address a group or an unfamiliar adult, the form changes significantly to reflect social distance.

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential in Ukrainian society. Even if the grammatical form remains direct, adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful in a café, a store, or on the street. It demonstrates good manners.

By mastering these short, effective phrases, you gain a vital tool for interacting directly with others and navigating daily situations in Ukrainian.

## Forming the Imperative: Singular and Informal

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends, family members, children, and people your own age in casual settings. We form this imperative by taking the verb and applying the command ending. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **чита́ти** | **чита́й** | read |
| **слу́хати** | **слу́хай** | listen |
| **чека́ти** | **чека́й** | wait |

For verbs like **чита́ти**, **слу́хати**, and **чека́ти**, the stem ends in a vowel. In these cases, we simply attach the letter **-й**. This creates a smooth sound that is easy to pronounce.

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.
> — **Чита́й ту́т.** — Read here.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). The **-и** ending ensures the word remains clear and distinguishable.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **писа́ти** | **пиши́** | write |
| **іти́** | **іди́** | go |
| **сказа́ти** | **скажи́** | say |
| **допомогти́** | **допоможи́** | help |
| **показа́ти** | **покажи́** | show |

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there) to give clear directions.

- **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
- **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
- **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
- **Покажи́ це́.** — Show this.

Let us see how people use these informal commands in everyday life. In this scenario, two friends are studying together and exchanging quick instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read this, please.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Диви́сь та́м.** — Look there.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

Notice how one person gives a command, and the other responds with a present tense verb describing their action. The form **пиши́** directly prompts the friend to act. It creates a natural, interactive dialogue.

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**). This phonetic rule helps maintain a natural flow of speech.

By remembering these two basic endings, you can build many commands for friends and family, allowing you to interact comfortably in relaxed environments.

## Forming the Imperative: Plural and Formal

When speaking to a group of people or addressing a single person respectfully, you use the formal or plural command form. This form corresponds directly to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You construct this formal imperative effortlessly by adding the suffix **-те** to the informal imperative you just learned.

| Informal Command (**ти**) | Formal/Plural Command (**ви**) | English |
|---|---|---|
| **чита́й** | **чита́йте** | read |
| **слу́хай** | **слу́хайте** | listen |
| **чека́й** | **чека́йте** | wait |
| **пиши́** | **пиші́ть** | write |
| **іди́** | **іді́ть** | go |
| **скажи́** | **скажі́ть** | say |

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажі́ть це́.** — Say this.

Using the **-те** suffix adds an instant layer of politeness and formality to your speech. If you are in a new place, speaking to an older person, or talking to a stranger on the street, you must choose the **ви** form. Failing to do so can sound extremely rude and disrespectful.

- **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
- **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
- **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
- **Чека́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Wait, please.
- **Слу́хайте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

We also use this form when a teacher addresses a classroom full of students, as the teacher is speaking to multiple people simultaneously.

> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.
> — **Ми чита́ємо.** — We are reading.

<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern in the formal imperative. For example, the verb **диви́тися** (to look) is reflexive. For the informal form, we use **диви́сь**. For the formal or plural form, we say **диві́ться**. Notice how the reflexive ending shifts slightly to accommodate the plural marker.

| Pronoun | Command | English |
|---|---|---|
| **ти** | **диви́сь** | look (informal) |
| **ви** | **диві́ться** | look (formal/plural) |

- **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
- **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
- **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. English uses the base verb, but Ukrainian requires you to adapt the verb into the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**) so it targets the person directly.

When you interact with native speakers in professional or public settings, paying close attention to the formal **-те** ending will greatly improve your communication skills and demonstrate your cultural awareness.

## Common Irregular Verbs and Aspect

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms that do not perfectly follow the standard patterns. You simply need to memorize them because they appear so often. The verbs **да́ти** (to give), **взя́ти** (to take), and **стоя́ти** (to stand) fall into this special group.

| Infinitive | Command (**ти**) | Command (**ви**) | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| **да́ти** | **да́й** | **да́йте** | give |
| **взя́ти** | **візьми́** | **візьмі́ть** | take |
| **стоя́ти** | **сті́й** | **сті́йте** | stand / stop |

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping, asking for directions, or navigating public spaces.

- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
- **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
- **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
- **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
- **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
- **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.

> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions and tell someone not to do something. We simply add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb. The verb form itself does not change.

- **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
- **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
- **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
- **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
- **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action. For example, the perfective verb **сказа́ти** asks for a specific, immediate answer, so the command **скажи́** focuses on the final result of the action rather than the continuous process.

Understanding these special verbs and negative structures allows you to express precise needs and boundaries.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions, make polite requests, and establish boundaries. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing, reflecting the social context of the conversation. You use the shorter form for informal interactions with friends (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite and respectful in any scenario.

- **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
- **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal/plural)
- **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
- **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal/plural)
- **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
- **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
- **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal/plural)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener before giving a command.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

It is highly practical for everyday life and represents the core language of action. > — **Та́к, я дивлю́сь.** — Yes, I am looking.
> — **Ду́же до́бре.** — Very good.

The examples above show direct instructions. You do not just describe the action; you actively prompt the other person to act. The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

- **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
- **Допоможи́!** — Help!
- **Покажи́!** — Show!
- **Чека́й!** — Wait!
- **Сті́й!** — Stop!
- **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
- **Чита́й це́!** — Read this!
- **Пиши́ ту́т!** — Write here!

Sometimes, you need to use the imperative mood for classroom instructions or when reacting to immediate, fast-paced events. The shortest commands are usually informal and direct.

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please). This single phrase instantly changes a strong order into a culturally appropriate request. You can place it at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.

- **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
- **Покажи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please show.

The mood relies heavily on the specific situation and social context. When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. When you address a group or an unfamiliar adult, the form changes significantly to reflect social distance.

<!-- SCOPE

Not covered:
  - Complex directions → a1-48
