resultDisplay.originalContent:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Form imperatives from required verbs, polite requests
Not covered:
  - Complex directions → a1-48
-->

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests. It is the language of action, heavily used in classrooms, streets, and daily conversations. Adding simple polite words transforms direct commands into respectful requests.

## Introduction: Asking for Action

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. It is highly practical for everyday life and represents the core language of action. In Ukrainian, the imperative mood is dynamic and frequently appears in classroom instructions, street conversations, and casual gatherings.

> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Listen, please!
> — **Та́к, я слу́хаю.** — Yes, I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Та́к, я дивлю́сь.** — Yes, I am looking.
> — **Ду́же до́бре.** — Very good.

The examples above show direct instructions. You do not just describe the action; you actively prompt the other person to act. The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

- **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
- **Допоможи́!** — Help!
- **Покажи́!** — Show!
- **Чека́й!** — Wait!
- **Сті́й!** — Stop!
- **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
- **Чита́й це́!** — Read this!
- **Пиши́ ту́т!** — Write here!

Sometimes, you need to use the imperative mood for classroom instructions or when reacting to immediate, fast-paced events. The shortest commands are usually informal and direct.

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please). This single phrase instantly changes a strong order into a culturally appropriate request. You can place it at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.

- **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
- **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
- **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
- **Покажи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please show.

The mood relies heavily on the specific situation and social context. When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. When you address a group or an unfamiliar adult, the form changes significantly to reflect social distance.

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential in Ukrainian society. Even if the grammatical form remains direct, adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful in a café, a store, or on the street. It demonstrates good manners.

By mastering these short, effective phrases, you gain a vital tool for interacting directly with others and navigating daily situations in Ukrainian.

## Forming the Imperative: Singular and Informal

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends, family members, children, and people your own age in casual settings. We form this imperative by taking the verb and applying the command ending. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

| Infinitive | Informal Command (**ти**) | Example Sentence | English Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **чита́ти** | **чита́й** | **Чита́й ту́т!** | Read here! |
| **слу́хати** | **слу́хай** | **Слу́хай це́!** | Listen to this! |
| **чека́ти** | **чека́й** | **Чека́й та́м!** | Wait there! |
| **писа́ти** | **пиши́** | **Пиши́ сло́во!** | Write the word! |
| **іти́** | **іди́** | **Іди́ ту́т!** | Go here! |
| **сказа́ти** | **скажи́** | **Скажи́ це́!** | Say this! |
| **допомогти́** | **допоможи́** | **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска!** | Help, please! |
| **показа́ти** | **покажи́** | **Покажи́ це́!** | Show this! |

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Та́к, я чита́ю.** — Yes, I am reading.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Я слу́хаю.** — I am listening.
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.
> — **Та́к, я чека́ю.** — Yes, I am waiting.
> — **Чита́й ту́т.** — Read here.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
> — **Покажи́ це́.** — Show this.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Диви́сь та́м.** — Look there.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

Notice how one person gives a command, and the other responds with a present tense verb describing their action. The form **пиши́** directly prompts the friend to act. It creates a natural, interactive dialogue.

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**). This phonetic rule helps maintain a natural flow of speech.

By remembering these two basic endings, you can build many commands for friends and family, allowing you to interact comfortably in relaxed environments.

## Forming the Imperative: Plural and Formal

When speaking to a group of people or addressing a single person respectfully, you use the formal or plural command form. This form corresponds directly to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You construct this formal imperative effortlessly by adding the suffix **-те** to the informal imperative you just learned.

| Informal Command (**ти**) | Formal Command (**ви**) | Formal Example Sentence | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| **чита́й** | **чита́йте** | **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска!** | Please read! |
| **слу́хай** | **слу́хайте** | **Слу́хайте це́!** | Listen to this! |
| **чека́й** | **чека́йте** | **Чека́йте та́м!** | Wait there! |
| **пиши́** | **пиші́ть** | **Пиші́ть сло́во!** | Write the word! |
| **іди́** | **іді́ть** | **Іді́ть ту́т!** | Go here! |
| **скажи́** | **скажі́ть** | **Скажі́ть це́!** | Say this! |
| **допоможи́**| **допоможі́ть**| **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** | Help, please! |
| **покажи́** | **покажі́ть** | **Покажі́ть це́!** | Show this! |

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Ми чита́ємо.** — We are reading.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажі́ть це́.** — Say this.
> — **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Чека́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Wait, please.
> — **Слу́хайте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

We also use this form when a teacher addresses a classroom full of students, as the teacher is speaking to multiple people simultaneously.

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern in the formal imperative. For example, the verb **диви́тися** (to look) is reflexive. For the informal form, we use **диви́сь**. For the formal or plural form, we say **диві́ться**. Notice how the reflexive ending shifts slightly to accommodate the plural marker.

| Pronoun | Command | English |
|---|---|---|
| **ти** | **диви́сь** | look (informal) |
| **ви** | **диві́ться** | look (formal/plural) |

- **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
- **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
- **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. English uses the base verb, but Ukrainian requires you to adapt the verb into the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**) so it targets the person directly.

When you interact with native speakers in professional or public settings, paying close attention to the formal **-те** ending will greatly improve your communication skills and demonstrate your cultural awareness.

## Common Irregular Verbs and Aspect

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms that do not perfectly follow the standard patterns. You simply need to memorize them because they appear so often. The verbs **да́ти** (to give), **взя́ти** (to take), and **стоя́ти** (to stand) fall into this special group.

| Infinitive | Command (**ти**) | Command (**ви**) | Example | English |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **да́ти** | **да́й** | **да́йте** | **Да́йте це́!** | Give this! |
| **взя́ти** | **візьми́** | **візьмі́ть** | **Візьмі́ть це́!** | Take this! |
| **стоя́ти** | **сті́й** | **сті́йте** | **Сті́йте та́м!** | Stand there! |

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping, asking for directions, or navigating public spaces.

> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
> — **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
> — **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
> — **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
> — **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
> — **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.
> — **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
> — **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
> — **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
> — **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
> — **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.
> — **Не да́й це́.** — Do not give this.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions and tell someone not to do something. We simply add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb. The verb form itself does not change.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action. For example, the perfective verb **сказа́ти** asks for a specific, immediate answer, so the command **скажи́** focuses on the final result of the action rather than the continuous process.

Understanding these special verbs and negative structures allows you to express precise needs and boundaries.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions, make polite requests, and establish boundaries. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing, reflecting the social context of the conversation. You use the shorter form for informal interactions with friends (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite and respectful in any scenario.

- **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
- **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal/plural)
- **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
- **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal/plural)
- **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
- **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
- **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
- **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal/plural)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener before giving a command.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). The **-и** ending ensures the word remains clear and distinguishable.

> — **Писа́ти** — to write
> — **Пиши́!** — Write!

> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Показа́ти** — to show
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Покажи́ це́!** — Show this!

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there) to give clear directions.

> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
> — **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Покажи́ це́.** — Show this.

Let us see how people use these informal commands in everyday life. In this scenario, two friends are studying together and exchanging quick instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read this, please.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Диви́сь та́м.** — Look there.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

Notice how one person gives a command, and the other responds with a present tense verb describing their action. The form **пиши́** directly prompts the friend to act. It creates a natural, interactive dialogue.

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**). This phonetic rule helps maintain a natural flow of speech.

By remembering these two basic endings, you can build many commands for friends and family, allowing you to interact comfortably in relaxed environments.

## Forming the Imperative: Plural and Formal

When speaking to a group of people or addressing a single person respectfully, you use the formal or plural command form. This form corresponds directly to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You construct this formal imperative effortlessly by adding the suffix **-те** to the informal imperative you just learned.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Please read!
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Слу́хайте це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait! (informal)
> — **Чека́йте!** — Wait! (formal)
> — **Чека́йте та́м!** — Wait there!
> — **Пиши́!** — Write! (informal)
> — **Пиші́ть!** — Write! (formal)
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іди́!** — Go! (informal)
> — **Іді́ть!** — Go! (formal)
> — **Іді́ть ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)
> — **Скажі́ть це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help! (informal)
> — **Допоможі́ть!** — Help! (formal)
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show! (informal)
> — **Покажі́ть!** — Show! (formal)
> — **Покажі́ть це́!** — Show this!

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажі́ть це́.** — Say this.

Using the **-те** suffix adds an instant layer of politeness and formality to your speech. If you are in a new place, speaking to an older person, or talking to a stranger on the street, you must choose the **ви** form. Failing to do so can sound extremely rude and disrespectful.

> — **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
> — **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Чека́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Wait, please.
> — **Слу́хайте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

We also use this form when a teacher addresses a classroom full of students, as the teacher is speaking to multiple people simultaneously.

> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.
> — **Ми чита́ємо.** — We are reading.

<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern in the formal imperative. For example, the verb **диви́тися** (to look) is reflexive. For the informal form, we use **диви́сь**. For the formal or plural form, we say **диві́ться**. Notice how the reflexive ending shifts slightly to accommodate the plural marker.

> — **Диви́сь!** — Look! (informal)
> — **Диві́ться!** — Look! (formal)
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. English uses the base verb, but Ukrainian requires you to adapt the verb into the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**) so it targets the person directly.

When you interact with native speakers in professional or public settings, paying close attention to the formal **-те** ending will greatly improve your communication skills and demonstrate your cultural awareness.

## Common Irregular Verbs and Aspect

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms that do not perfectly follow the standard patterns. You simply need to memorize them because they appear so often. The verbs **да́ти** (to give), **взя́ти** (to take), and **стоя́ти** (to stand) fall into this special group.

> — **Да́ти** — to give
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Да́йте це́!** — Give this!
> — **Взя́ти** — to take
> — **Візьми́!** — Take! (informal)
> — **Візьмі́ть!** — Take! (formal)
> — **Візьмі́ть це́!** — Take this!
> — **Стоя́ти** — to stand / stop
> — **Сті́й!** — Stand! (informal)
> — **Сті́йте!** — Stand! (formal)
> — **Сті́йте та́м!** — Stand there!

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping, asking for directions, or navigating public spaces.

> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
> — **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
> — **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
> — **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
> — **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
> — **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.
> — **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
> — **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
> — **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
> — **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
> — **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.
> — **Не да́й це́.** — Do not give this.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions and tell someone not to do something. We simply add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb. The verb form itself does not change.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action. For example, the perfective verb **сказа́ти** asks for a specific, immediate answer, so the command **скажи́** focuses on the final result of the action rather than the continuous process.

Understanding these special verbs and negative structures allows you to express precise needs and boundaries.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions, make polite requests, and establish boundaries. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing, reflecting the social context of the conversation. You use the shorter form for informal interactions with friends (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite and respectful in any scenario.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener before giving a command.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Form imperatives from required verbs, polite requests
Not covered:
  - Complex directions → a1-48
-->

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests.

## Introduction: Asking for Action

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. 

> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Listen, please!
> — **Та́к, я слу́хаю.** — Yes, I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Та́к, я дивлю́сь.** — Yes, I am looking.
> — **Ду́же до́бре.** — Very good.
> — **Чита́й це́.** — Read this.
> — **Та́к, я чита́ю.** — Yes, I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.

The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

> — **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Сті́й!** — Stop!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Чита́й це́!** — Read this!
> — **Пиши́ ту́т!** — Write here!

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please).

> — **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Покажи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please show.
> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please listen.
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Пиши́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please write.
> — **Чека́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please wait.
> — **Іди́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please go.
> — **Диви́сь, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Сті́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please stop.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please help.

When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. 

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential. Adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful.

## Forming the Imperative: Singular and Informal

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends and family. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

For verbs like **чита́ти**, **слу́хати**, and **чека́ти**, the stem ends in a vowel. We attach the letter **-й**.

> — **Чита́ти** — to read
> — **Чита́й!** — Read!
> — **Чита́й ту́т!** — Read here!
> — **Слу́хати** — to listen
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Слу́хай це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́ти** — to wait
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чека́й та́м!** — Wait there!

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.
> — **Чита́й ту́т.** — Read here.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read, please.
> — **Слу́хай, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). 

> — **Писа́ти** — to write
> — **Пиши́!** — Write!
> — **Пиши́ сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іти́** — to go
> — **Іди́!** — Go!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Сказа́ти** — to say
> — **Скажи́!** — Say!
> — **Скажи́ це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допомогти́** — to help
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Показа́ти** — to show
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Покажи́ це́!** — Show this!

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there).

> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
> — **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Покажи́ це́.** — Show this.
> — **Пиши́ та́м.** — Write there.
> — **Іди́ ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажи́ це́.** — Say this.

In this scenario, two friends are exchanging quick instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read this, please.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Диви́сь та́м.** — Look there.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.

> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажі́ть це́.** — Say this.

Using the **-те** suffix adds politeness. If you are talking to a stranger, you must choose the **ви** form. 

> — **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?

> — **Взя́ти** — to take

> — **Візьмі́ть!** — Take! (formal)
> — **Візьмі́ть це́!** — Take this!
> — **Візьми́ це́!** — Take this!
> — **Стоя́ти** — to stand / stop
> — **Сті́й!** — Stand! (informal)
> — **Сті́йте!** — Stand! (formal)
> — **Сті́йте та́м!** — Stand there!
> — **Сті́й ту́т!** — Stand here!

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping or asking for directions.

> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
> — **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
> — **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
> — **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
> — **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
> — **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.

> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions. Add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb.

> — **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
> — **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
> — **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
> — **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
> — **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.
> — **Не да́й це́.** — Do not give this.
> — **Не чека́й.** — Do not wait.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action, like **скажи́**.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions and make polite requests. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing. You use the shorter form for informal interactions (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Form imperatives from required verbs, polite requests
Not covered:
  - Complex directions → a1-48
-->

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests. It is the language of action. Adding simple polite words transforms direct commands into respectful requests.

## Introduction: Asking for Action

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. It is highly practical for everyday life and represents the core language of action. In Ukrainian, the imperative mood is dynamic and frequently appears in classroom instructions, street conversations, and casual gatherings.

> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Listen, please!
> — **Та́к, я слу́хаю.** — Yes, I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Та́к, я дивлю́сь.** — Yes, I am looking.
> — **Ду́же до́бре.** — Very good.
> — **Чита́й це́.** — Read this.
> — **Та́к, я чита́ю.** — Yes, I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.

The examples above show direct instructions. You do not just describe the action; you actively prompt the other person to act. The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

> — **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Сті́й!** — Stop!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Чита́й це́!** — Read this!
> — **Пиши́ ту́т!** — Write here!

Sometimes, you need to use the imperative mood for classroom instructions or when reacting to immediate, fast-paced events. The shortest commands are usually informal and direct.

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please). This single phrase instantly changes a strong order into a culturally appropriate request. You can place it at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.

> — **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Покажи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please show.
> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please listen.
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Пиши́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please write.
> — **Чека́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please wait.
> — **Іди́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please go.
> — **Диви́сь, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.

The mood relies heavily on the specific situation and social context. When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. When you address a group or an unfamiliar adult, the form changes significantly to reflect social distance.

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential in Ukrainian society. Even if the grammatical form remains direct, adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful in a café, a store, or on the street. It demonstrates good manners.

By mastering these short, effective phrases, you gain a vital tool for interacting directly with others and navigating daily situations in Ukrainian.

## Forming the Imperative: Singular and Informal

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends, family members, children, and people your own age in casual settings. We form this imperative by taking the verb and applying the command ending. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

For verbs like **чита́ти**, **слу́хати**, and **чека́ти**, the stem ends in a vowel. In these cases, we simply attach the letter **-й**. This creates a smooth sound that is easy to pronounce.

> — **Чита́ти** — to read
> — **Чита́й!** — Read!
> — **Чита́й ту́т!** — Read here!
> — **Слу́хати** — to listen
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Слу́хай це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́ти** — to wait
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чека́й та́м!** — Wait there!

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.
> — **Чита́й ту́т.** — Read here.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). The **-и** ending ensures the word remains clear and distinguishable.

> — **Писа́ти** — to write
> — **Пиши́!** — Write!
> — **Пиши́ сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іти́** — to go
> — **Іди́!** — Go!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Сказа́ти** — to say
> — **Скажи́!** — Say!
> — **Скажи́ це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допомогти́** — to help
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Показа́ти** — to show
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Покажи́ це́!** — Show this!

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there) to give clear directions.

> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
> — **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Покажи́ це́.** — Show this.

Let us see how people use these informal commands in everyday life. In this scenario, two friends are studying together and exchanging quick instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read this, please.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Диви́сь та́м.** — Look there.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

Notice how one person gives a command, and the other responds with a present tense verb describing their action. The form **пиши́** directly prompts the friend to act. It creates a natural, interactive dialogue.

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**). This phonetic rule helps maintain a natural flow of speech.

By remembering these two basic endings, you can build many commands for friends and family, allowing you to interact comfortably in relaxed environments.

## Forming the Imperative: Plural and Formal

When speaking to a group of people or addressing a single person respectfully, you use the formal or plural command form. This form corresponds directly to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You construct this formal imperative effortlessly by adding the suffix **-те** to the informal imperative you just learned.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Please read!
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Слу́хайте це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait! (informal)
> — **Чека́йте!** — Wait! (formal)
> — **Чека́йте та́м!** — Wait there!
> — **Пиши́!** — Write! (informal)
> — **Пиші́ть!** — Write! (formal)
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іди́!** — Go! (informal)
> — **Іді́ть!** — Go! (formal)
> — **Іді́ть ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)
> — **Скажі́ть це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help! (informal)
> — **Допоможі́ть!** — Help! (formal)
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show! (informal)
> — **Покажі́ть!** — Show! (formal)
> — **Покажі́ть це́!** — Show this!

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажі́ть це́.** — Say this.

Using the **-те** suffix adds an instant layer of politeness and formality to your speech. If you are in a new place, speaking to an older person, or talking to a stranger on the street, you must choose the **ви** form. Failing to do so can sound extremely rude and disrespectful.

> — **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
> — **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Чека́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Wait, please.
> — **Слу́хайте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

We also use this form when a teacher addresses a classroom full of students, as the teacher is speaking to multiple people simultaneously.

> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.
> — **Ми чита́ємо.** — We are reading.

<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern in the formal imperative. For example, the verb **диви́тися** (to look) is reflexive. For the informal form, we use **диви́сь**. For the formal or plural form, we say **диві́ться**. Notice how the reflexive ending shifts slightly to accommodate the plural marker.

> — **Диви́сь!** — Look! (informal)
> — **Диві́ться!** — Look! (formal)
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. English uses the base verb, but Ukrainian requires you to adapt the verb into the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**) so it targets the person directly.

When you interact with native speakers in professional or public settings, paying close attention to the formal **-те** ending will greatly improve your communication skills and demonstrate your cultural awareness.

## Common Irregular Verbs and Aspect

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms that do not perfectly follow the standard patterns. You simply need to memorize them because they appear so often. The verbs **да́ти** (to give), **взя́ти** (to take), and **стоя́ти** (to stand) fall into this special group.

> — **Да́ти** — to give
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Да́йте це́!** — Give this!
> — **Взя́ти** — to take
> — **Візьми́!** — Take! (informal)
> — **Візьмі́ть!** — Take! (formal)
> — **Візьмі́ть це́!** — Take this!
> — **Стоя́ти** — to stand / stop
> — **Сті́й!** — Stand! (informal)
> — **Сті́йте!** — Stand! (formal)
> — **Сті́йте та́м!** — Stand there!

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping, asking for directions, or navigating public spaces.

> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
> — **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
> — **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
> — **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
> — **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
> — **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.
> — **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
> — **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
> — **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
> — **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
> — **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.
> — **Не да́й це́.** — Do not give this.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions and tell someone not to do something. We simply add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb. The verb form itself does not change.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action. For example, the perfective verb **сказа́ти** asks for a specific, immediate answer, so the command **скажи́** focuses on the final result of the action rather than the continuous process.

Understanding these special verbs and negative structures allows you to express precise needs and boundaries.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions, make polite requests, and establish boundaries. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing, reflecting the social context of the conversation. You use the shorter form for informal interactions with friends (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite and respectful in any scenario.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener before giving a command.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**
resultDisplay.newContent:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Form imperatives from required verbs, polite requests
Not covered:
  - Complex directions → a1-48
-->

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests.

## Introduction: Asking for Action

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. 

> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Listen, please!
> — **Та́к, я слу́хаю.** — Yes, I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Та́к, я дивлю́сь.** — Yes, I am looking.
> — **Ду́же до́бре.** — Very good.
> — **Чита́й це́.** — Read this.
> — **Та́к, я чита́ю.** — Yes, I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.

The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

> — **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Сті́й!** — Stop!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Чита́й це́!** — Read this!
> — **Пиши́ ту́т!** — Write here!

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please).

> — **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Покажи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please show.
> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please listen.
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Пиши́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please write.
> — **Чека́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please wait.
> — **Іди́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please go.
> — **Диви́сь, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Сті́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please stop.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please help.

When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. 

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential. Adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful.

## Forming the Imperative: Singular and Informal

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends and family. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

For verbs like **чита́ти**, **слу́хати**, and **чека́ти**, the stem ends in a vowel. We attach the letter **-й**.

> — **Чита́ти** — to read
> — **Чита́й!** — Read!
> — **Чита́й ту́т!** — Read here!
> — **Слу́хати** — to listen
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Слу́хай це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́ти** — to wait
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чека́й та́м!** — Wait there!

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.
> — **Чита́й ту́т.** — Read here.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read, please.
> — **Слу́хай, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). 

> — **Писа́ти** — to write
> — **Пиши́!** — Write!
> — **Пиши́ сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іти́** — to go
> — **Іди́!** — Go!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Сказа́ти** — to say
> — **Скажи́!** — Say!
> — **Скажи́ це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допомогти́** — to help
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Показа́ти** — to show
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Покажи́ це́!** — Show this!

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there).

> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
> — **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Покажи́ це́.** — Show this.
> — **Пиши́ та́м.** — Write there.
> — **Іди́ ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажи́ це́.** — Say this.

In this scenario, two friends are exchanging quick instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read this, please.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Диви́сь та́м.** — Look there.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Я слу́хаю.** — I am listening.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**).

## Forming the Imperative: Plural and Formal

When speaking to a group of people or respectfully to one person, you use the formal or plural form. This form corresponds to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You add the suffix **-те**.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Please read!
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Слу́хайте це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait! (informal)
> — **Чека́йте!** — Wait! (formal)
> — **Чека́йте та́м!** — Wait there!
> — **Пиши́!** — Write! (informal)
> — **Пиші́ть!** — Write! (formal)
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іди́!** — Go! (informal)
> — **Іді́ть!** — Go! (formal)
> — **Іді́ть ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)
> — **Скажі́ть це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help! (informal)
> — **Допоможі́ть!** — Help! (formal)
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show! (informal)
> — **Покажі́ть!** — Show! (formal)
> — **Покажі́ть це́!** — Show this!

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажі́ть це́.** — Say this.

Using the **-те** suffix adds politeness. If you are talking to a stranger, you must choose the **ви** form. 

> — **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
> — **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Чека́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Wait, please.
> — **Слу́хайте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

We use this form when addressing a classroom full of students.

> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.
> — **Ми чита́ємо.** — We are reading.

<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern. For the verb **диви́тися** (to look), we use **диви́сь** (informal) and **диві́ться** (formal/plural).

> — **Диви́сь!** — Look! (informal)
> — **Диві́ться!** — Look! (formal)
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. Always use the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**).

## Common Irregular Verbs and Aspect

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms. You simply need to memorize them.

> — **Да́ти** — to give
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Да́йте це́!** — Give this!
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Give, please!
> — **Взя́ти** — to take
> — **Візьми́!** — Take! (informal)
> — **Візьмі́ть!** — Take! (formal)
> — **Візьмі́ть це́!** — Take this!
> — **Візьми́ це́!** — Take this!
> — **Стоя́ти** — to stand / stop
> — **Сті́й!** — Stand! (informal)
> — **Сті́йте!** — Stand! (formal)
> — **Сті́йте та́м!** — Stand there!
> — **Сті́й ту́т!** — Stand here!

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping or asking for directions.

> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
> — **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
> — **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
> — **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
> — **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
> — **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions. Add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb.

> — **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
> — **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
> — **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
> — **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
> — **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.
> — **Не да́й це́.** — Do not give this.
> — **Не чека́й.** — Do not wait.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action, like **скажи́**.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions and make polite requests. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing. You use the shorter form for informal interactions (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Form imperatives from required verbs, polite requests
Not covered:
  - Complex directions → a1-48
-->

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests.

## Introduction: Asking for Action

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. 

> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Listen, please!
> — **Та́к, я слу́хаю.** — Yes, I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Та́к, я дивлю́сь.** — Yes, I am looking.
> — **Ду́же до́бре.** — Very good.
> — **Чита́й це́.** — Read this.
> — **Та́к, я чита́ю.** — Yes, I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.

The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

> — **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Сті́й!** — Stop!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Чита́й це́!** — Read this!
> — **Пиши́ ту́т!** — Write here!

Use **бу́дь ла́ска** (please) to make commands polite.

> — **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Покажи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please show.
> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please listen.
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Пиши́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please write.
> — **Чека́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please wait.
> — **Іди́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please go.
> — **Диви́сь, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Сті́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please stop.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please help.

When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. 

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential. Adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful.

## Forming the Imperative: Singular and Informal

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends and family. For many verbs, drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

For stems ending in a vowel (**чита́ти**, **слу́хати**, **чека́ти**), add **-й**.

> — **Чита́ти** — to read
> — **Чита́й!** — Read!
> — **Чита́й ту́т!** — Read here!
> — **Слу́хати** — to listen
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Слу́хай це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́ти** — to wait
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чека́й та́м!** — Wait there!

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.
> — **Чита́й ту́т.** — Read here.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read, please.
> — **Слу́хай, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

For stems ending in a consonant (**писа́ти**, **іти́**), add **-и**.

> — **Писа́ти** — to write
> — **Пиши́!** — Write!
> — **Пиши́ сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іти́** — to go
> — **Іди́!** — Go!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Сказа́ти** — to say
> — **Скажи́!** — Say!
> — **Скажи́ це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допомогти́** — to help
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Показа́ти** — to show
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Покажи́ це́!** — Show this!

Combine verbs with **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there).

> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
> — **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Покажи́ це́.** — Show this.
> — **Пиши́ та́м.** — Write there.
> — **Іди́ ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажи́ це́.** — Say this.

In this scenario, two friends are exchanging instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read this, please.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Диви́сь та́м.** — Look there.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Я слу́хаю.** — I am listening.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**).

## Forming the Imperative: Plural and Formal

When speaking to a group of people or respectfully to one person, you use the formal or plural form. This form corresponds to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You add the suffix **-те**.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Please read!
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Слу́хайте це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait! (informal)
> — **Чека́йте!** — Wait! (formal)
> — **Чека́йте та́м!** — Wait there!
> — **Пиши́!** — Write! (informal)
> — **Пиші́ть!** — Write! (formal)
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іди́!** — Go! (informal)
> — **Іді́ть!** — Go! (formal)
> — **Іді́ть ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)
> — **Скажі́ть це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help! (informal)
> — **Допоможі́ть!** — Help! (formal)
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show! (informal)
> — **Покажі́ть!** — Show! (formal)
> — **Покажі́ть це́!** — Show this!

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажі́ть це́.** — Say this.

Using the **-те** suffix adds politeness. If you are talking to a stranger, you must choose the **ви** form. 

> — **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
> — **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Чека́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Wait, please.
> — **Слу́хайте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

We use this form when addressing a classroom full of students.

> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.
> — **Ми чита́ємо.** — We are reading.

<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern. For the verb **диви́тися** (to look), we use **диви́сь** (informal) and **диві́ться** (formal/plural).

> — **Диви́сь!** — Look! (informal)
> — **Диві́ться!** — Look! (formal)
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. Always use the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**).

## Common Irregular Verbs and Aspect

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms. You simply need to memorize them.

> — **Да́ти** — to give
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Да́йте це́!** — Give this!
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Give, please!
> — **Взя́ти** — to take
> — **Візьми́!** — Take! (informal)
> — **Візьмі́ть!** — Take! (formal)
> — **Візьмі́ть це́!** — Take this!
> — **Візьми́ це́!** — Take this!
> — **Стоя́ти** — to stand / stop
> — **Сті́й!** — Stand! (informal)
> — **Сті́йте!** — Stand! (formal)
> — **Сті́йте та́м!** — Stand there!
> — **Сті́й ту́т!** — Stand here!

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping or asking for directions.

> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
> — **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
> — **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
> — **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
> — **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
> — **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions. Add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb.

> — **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
> — **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
> — **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
> — **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
> — **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.
> — **Не да́й це́.** — Do not give this.
> — **Не чека́й.** — Do not wait.
> — **Не скажи́ це́.** — Do not say this.
> — **Не сті́й ту́т.** — Do not stand here.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action, like **скажи́**.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions and make polite requests. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing. You use the shorter form for informal interactions (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

<!-- SCOPE

Not covered:

# Imperative and Requests

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> The imperative mood allows you to ask for help, give instructions, or make requests.

## Introduction: Asking for Action

We use the imperative mood when we want someone to do something. This includes giving direct instructions, asking for a favor, or seeking help. 

> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Listen, please!
> — **Та́к, я слу́хаю.** — Yes, I am listening.
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Та́к, я дивлю́сь.** — Yes, I am looking.
> — **Ду́же до́бре.** — Very good.
> — **Чита́й це́.** — Read this.
> — **Та́к, я чита́ю.** — Yes, I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.

The imperative sets an expectation that the listener will respond.

> — **Да́й це́!** — Give this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Сті́й!** — Stop!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Чита́й це́!** — Read this!
> — **Пиши́ ту́т!** — Write here!

To soften a command and make it polite, we frequently include the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** (please).

> — **Скажи́, бу́дь ла́ска, де́ ві́н?** — Please tell, where is he?
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Покажи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please show.
> — **Слуха́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please listen.
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Пиши́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please write.
> — **Чека́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please wait.
> — **Іди́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please go.
> — **Диви́сь, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Сті́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please stop.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please help.

When you say **чита́й** (read), you give an instruction to one person you know well. 

> [!culture] Politeness Matters
> Using **бу́дь ла́ска** is essential. Adding this simple phrase makes your speech polite and respectful.

## Forming the Imperative: Singular and Informal

The informal singular imperative addresses someone you call **ти** (you, informal). You will use this form with friends and family. For many common verbs, you drop the infinitive ending and add **-й** or **-и**.

For verbs like **чита́ти**, **слу́хати**, and **чека́ти**, the stem ends in a vowel. We attach the letter **-й**.

> — **Чита́ти** — to read
> — **Чита́й!** — Read!
> — **Чита́й ту́т!** — Read here!
> — **Слу́хати** — to listen
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Слу́хай це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́ти** — to wait
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чека́й та́м!** — Wait there!

> — **Чита́й сло́во.** — Read the word.
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen!
> — **Чека́й ту́т.** — Wait here.
> — **Чита́й ту́т.** — Read here.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait!
> — **Чита́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read, please.
> — **Слу́хай, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

For other verbs, the stem ends in a consonant, and the ending becomes **-и**. This applies to verbs like **писа́ти** (to write) and **іти́** (to go). 

> — **Писа́ти** — to write
> — **Пиши́!** — Write!
> — **Пиши́ сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іти́** — to go
> — **Іди́!** — Go!
> — **Іди́ ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Сказа́ти** — to say
> — **Скажи́!** — Say!
> — **Скажи́ це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допомогти́** — to help
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help!
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Показа́ти** — to show
> — **Покажи́!** — Show!
> — **Покажи́ це́!** — Show this!

You can form simple, direct sentences with these verbs. We often combine them with adverbs of place like **ту́т** (here) or **та́м** (there).

> — **Пиши́ ту́т.** — Write here.
> — **Іди́ та́м.** — Go there.
> — **Скажи́ сло́во.** — Say the word.
> — **Допоможи́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Help, please.
> — **Покажи́ це́.** — Show this.
> — **Пиши́ та́м.** — Write there.
> — **Іди́ ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажи́ це́.** — Say this.

In this scenario, two friends are exchanging quick instructions.

> — **Чита́й це́, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Read this, please.
> — **Я чита́ю.** — I am reading.
> — **Пиши́ сло́во ту́т.** — Write the word here.
> — **Та́к, я пишу́.** — Yes, I am writing.
> — **Диви́сь та́м.** — Look there.
> — **Я дивлю́сь.** — I am looking.
> — **Слу́хай це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Я слу́хаю.** — I am listening.

<!-- adapted from: textbook conversational scenarios -->

> [!tip] Watch the Endings
> If the verb stem ends in a vowel, use **-й** (**чита́й**). If it ends in a consonant, use **-и** (**пиши́**).

## Forming the Imperative: Plural and Formal

When speaking to a group of people or respectfully to one person, you use the formal or plural form. This form corresponds to the pronoun **ви** (you, formal/plural). You add the suffix **-те**.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Please read!
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Слу́хайте це́!** — Listen to this!
> — **Чека́й!** — Wait! (informal)
> — **Чека́йте!** — Wait! (formal)
> — **Чека́йте та́м!** — Wait there!
> — **Пиши́!** — Write! (informal)
> — **Пиші́ть!** — Write! (formal)
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во!** — Write the word!
> — **Іди́!** — Go! (informal)
> — **Іді́ть!** — Go! (formal)
> — **Іді́ть ту́т!** — Go here!
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)
> — **Скажі́ть це́!** — Say this!
> — **Допоможи́!** — Help! (informal)
> — **Допоможі́ть!** — Help! (formal)
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Покажи́!** — Show! (informal)
> — **Покажі́ть!** — Show! (formal)
> — **Покажі́ть це́!** — Show this!

> — **Чита́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please read.
> — **Слу́хайте це́.** — Listen to this.
> — **Чека́йте та́м.** — Wait there.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Іді́ть ту́т.** — Go here.
> — **Скажі́ть це́.** — Say this.

Using the **-те** suffix adds politeness. If you are talking to a stranger, you must choose the **ви** form. 

> — **Скажі́ть, де́ ві́н?** — Tell me, where is he?
> — **Покажі́ть це́.** — Show this.
> — **Допоможі́ть, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Help, please!
> — **Чека́йте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Wait, please.
> — **Слу́хайте, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Listen, please.

We use this form when addressing a classroom full of students.

> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen!
> — **Ми слу́хаємо.** — We are listening.
> — **Пиші́ть сло́во.** — Write the word.
> — **Ми пи́шемо.** — We are writing.
> — **Чита́йте та́кож.** — Read also.
> — **Ми чита́ємо.** — We are reading.

<!-- adapted from: ponomarova Grade 3 -->

Reflexive verbs maintain a unique pattern. For the verb **диви́тися** (to look), we use **диви́сь** (informal) and **диві́ться** (formal/plural).

> — **Диви́сь!** — Look! (informal)
> — **Диві́ться!** — Look! (formal)
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. Always use the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**).

## Common Irregular Verbs and Aspect

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms. You simply need to memorize them.

> — **Да́ти** — to give
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Да́йте це́!** — Give this!
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Give, please!
> — **Взя́ти** — to take
> — **Візьми́!** — Take! (informal)
> — **Візьмі́ть!** — Take! (formal)
> — **Візьмі́ть це́!** — Take this!
> — **Візьми́ це́!** — Take this!
> — **Стоя́ти** — to stand / stop
> — **Сті́й!** — Stand! (informal)
> — **Сті́йте!** — Stand! (formal)
> — **Сті́йте та́м!** — Stand there!
> — **Сті́й ту́т!** — Stand here!

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping or asking for directions.

> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
> — **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
> — **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
> — **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
> — **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
> — **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions. Add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb.

> — **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
> — **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
> — **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
> — **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
> — **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.
> — **Не да́й це́.** — Do not give this.
> — **Не чека́й.** — Do not wait.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action, like **скажи́**.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions and make polite requests. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing. You use the shorter form for informal interactions (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**

> — **Диві́ться!** — Look! (formal)
> — **Диви́сь ту́т.** — Look here.
> — **Диві́ться, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Please look.
> — **Диві́ться та́м.** — Look there.

> [!warning] A Common Learner Error
> Do not use the infinitive (like ~~чита́ти~~) when giving a personal command. Always use the proper imperative form (**чита́й** or **чита́йте**).

## Common Irregular Verbs and Aspect

Some high-frequency verbs feature irregular command forms. You simply need to memorize them.

> — **Да́ти** — to give
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Да́йте це́!** — Give this!
> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска!** — Give, please!
> — **Взя́ти** — to take
> — **Візьми́!** — Take! (informal)
> — **Візьмі́ть!** — Take! (formal)
> — **Візьмі́ть це́!** — Take this!
> — **Візьми́ це́!** — Take this!
> — **Стоя́ти** — to stand / stop
> — **Сті́й!** — Stand! (informal)
> — **Сті́йте!** — Stand! (formal)
> — **Сті́йте та́м!** — Stand there!
> — **Сті́й ту́т!** — Stand here!

You will hear these commands constantly in daily life, especially when shopping or asking for directions.

> — **Да́й, бу́дь ла́ска.** — Give, please.
> — **Візьмі́ть це́.** — Take this.
> — **Сті́йте та́м.** — Stand there.
> — **Да́йте сло́во.** — Give the word.
> — **Візьми́ ча́с.** — Take time.
> — **Сті́й ту́т.** — Stand here.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, це́.** — Please give this.
> — **Візьмі́ть.** — Take it.
> — **А це́ яки́й ча́с?** — And what time is this?
> — **Чека́йте.** — Wait.
> — **Да́йте, бу́дь ла́ска, ча́с.** — Please give time.
> — **Чека́ю.** — I am waiting.

<!-- original: no matching textbook exercise found -->

You can also use negative commands to establish clear prohibitions. Add the negative word **не** (not) directly before the imperative verb.

> — **Не чита́й це́.** — Do not read this.
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write.
> — **Не диви́сь.** — Do not look.
> — **Не слу́хайте.** — Do not listen.
> — **Не іди́ та́м.** — Do not go there.
> — **Не візьми́ це́.** — Do not take this.
> — **Не да́й це́.** — Do not give this.
> — **Не чека́й.** — Do not wait.
> — **Не скажи́ це́.** — Do not say this.
> — **Не сті́й ту́т.** — Do not stand here.

> [!did-you-know] Direct Action
> Ukrainian sometimes uses the perfective aspect in a command when requesting a single, completed action, like **скажи́**.

## Summary and Self-Check

The imperative mood allows you to give direct instructions and make polite requests. The grammatical form changes depending on who you are addressing. You use the shorter form for informal interactions (**ти**) and attach the **-те** suffix for formal or plural situations (**ви**). Adding the phrase **бу́дь ла́ска** ensures you remain polite.

> — **Чита́й!** — Read! (informal)
> — **Чита́йте!** — Read! (formal)
> — **Слу́хай!** — Listen! (informal)
> — **Слу́хайте!** — Listen! (formal)
> — **Не пиши́.** — Do not write. (informal negative)
> — **Не пиші́ть.** — Do not write. (formal negative)
> — **Да́й!** — Give! (informal)
> — **Да́йте!** — Give! (formal)
> — **Скажи́!** — Say! (informal)
> — **Скажі́ть!** — Say! (formal)

Review these rules and practice using both the formal and informal forms. Always remember to assess your relationship with the listener.

---

# Підсумок

The imperative mood allows you to give commands and make requests. You use the informal form for **ти** and add **-те** for the formal/plural **ви**.

- Як сказати «Read!» формально? (How do you say "Read!" formally?) — **Чита́йте!**
- Як сказати «Listen!» неформально? (How do you say "Listen!" informally?) — **Слу́хай!**
- Як зробити прохання ввічливим? (How do you make a request polite?) — Add **бу́дь ла́ска**.
- Як сказати «Look!» формально? (How do you say "Look!" formally?) — **Диві́ться!**
